coredata.dc:title: An eco-friendly sensor based on CQD@MIPs for detection of N-acylated homoserine lactones and its 3D printing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xingxing
Sun, Jiadi
Cui, Fangchao
Ji, Jian
Wang, Liping
Zhang, Yinzhi
Sun, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: N-acylated homoserine lactones (AHLs), a class of auto-inducers produced by Gram-negative bacteria, are typical signaling molecules in quorum sensing (QS) systems. Importantly, AHLs play a key role in determining the virulence of foodborne pathogens and reflect the activity of spoilage bacteria. In this study, an eco-friendly fluorescence-sensing platform for the rapid and sensitive detection of AHLs was developed and characterized. Molecularly imprinted polymers embedded with yellow-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via the sol-gel process using furanone as an alternative template molecule, and long-wave-emitting CQDs with excellent optical properties were used as signal conversion materials. After template elution, the blotting cavities on the surface of the CQD@MIPs (molecularly imprinted polymers) were able to selectively recognize AHLs, demonstrating a stronger fluorescence response compared with the corresponding CQD@NIPs (non-imprinted polymers). Under optimal test conditions, a good linear relationship between the concentration of analyte and the relative fluorescence intensity of the CQD@MIPs was observed. The linear detection range was 0–2.0 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.067 μM. Importantly, the proposed sensing platform functioned as an optical detection strategy that responded quickly (2 min) to AHLs. Additionally, this sensing platform was applied to the analysis of AHLs in bacterial supernatant samples with satisfactory results. More interestingly, the 3D-printing CQD@MIPs were tentative explored in this work, which was personalized and portable, has an advantage of point of care testing (POCT) detection in the future. Based on these results, this detection strategy has demonstrated substantial potential for application in and the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-acylated homoserine lactones
Fluorescence-sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the CQD@MIPs sensing strategy
Fluorometric sensing of analyte by CQD@MIPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National First-class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology
State Key Laboratory of Dairy Biotechnology
Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the National Research Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602800 ), Jiangsu Agriculture Innovation Fund ( CX (17) 3007 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190584 ), Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Dairy Biotechnology (No. SKLDB2019-005 ), National First-class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180303 ), Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized FL spectra of CQDs and CQD@MIPs at 500 nm as excitation wavelength. (B) FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs (a), CQD@MIPs (b) and MIPs without embedded CQDs (c).

(A) Confirmation of the removal of the template from CQD@MIPs cavities by UV–vis spectroscopy. (B) FT-IR spectrum of DMHF (a), CQD@NIPs (b), CQD@MIPs after (c) and before (d) template molecules extraction. (C) FL spectra of (a) CQD@NIPs, CQD@NIPs with addition of DMHF (b), CQD@MIPs after extraction (c), CQD@MIPs with addition of DMHF (d) and CQD@MIPs before extraction (e).
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of CQD@MIPs (A) and CQD@NIPs (B) and their corresponding high-resolution insets.

FL spectra of CQD@MIPs (A) and CQD@NIPs (B) with addition of indicated concentrations of template molecules and related Stern-Volmer plots for CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs with different concentrations of DMHF in test solution (C).

(A) Fluorescence response of the CQD@MIP and CQD@NIP by various kinds of AHL (A: C4-HSL, B: C6-HSL, C: 3-O-C6-(L)-HSL, and D: C8-HSL) and other compounds (E: maltol, F: phthalide and G: tremetone). (B) Based on the proposed sensing platform for detecting AHLs from various bacterial supernatants (A: Escherichia coli, B: Aeromonas hydrophila, C: Pseudomonas fluorescens, D: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and E: Staphylococcus aureus).
Photographs of the 3D-printing CQD@MIPs with the addition of pure water (A) and 20 μM of DMHF aqueous solution (B) under 450 nm LED lamp irradiation.
Schematic of construction of the CQD@MIPs sensing strategy.
Summarizing the analytical parameters of Stern-Volmer constant and imprinting factor of CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs for AHLs.

head.ce:title: An eco-friendly sensor based on CQD@MIPs for detection of N-acylated homoserine lactones and its 3D printing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Foods, School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center for Functional Foods
School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Foods, School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-acylated homoserine lactones (AHLs), a class of auto-inducers produced by Gram-negative bacteria, are typical signaling molecules in quorum sensing (QS) systems. Importantly, AHLs play a key role in determining the virulence of foodborne pathogens and reflect the activity of spoilage bacteria. In this study, an eco-friendly fluorescence-sensing platform for the rapid and sensitive detection of AHLs was developed and characterized. Molecularly imprinted polymers embedded with yellow-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via the sol-gel process using furanone as an alternative template molecule, and long-wave-emitting CQDs with excellent optical properties were used as signal conversion materials. After template elution, the blotting cavities on the surface of the CQD@MIPs (molecularly imprinted polymers) were able to selectively recognize AHLs, demonstrating a stronger fluorescence response compared with the corresponding CQD@NIPs (non-imprinted polymers). Under optimal test conditions, a good linear relationship between the concentration of analyte and the relative fluorescence intensity of the CQD@MIPs was observed. The linear detection range was 0–2.0 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.067 μM. Importantly, the proposed sensing platform functioned as an optical detection strategy that responded quickly (2 min) to AHLs. Additionally, this sensing platform was applied to the analysis of AHLs in bacterial supernatant samples with satisfactory results. More interestingly, the 3D-printing CQD@MIPs were tentative explored in this work, which was personalized and portable, has an advantage of point of care testing (POCT) detection in the future. Based on these results, this detection strategy has demonstrated substantial potential for application in and the field of food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogens and spoilage bacteria are crucial hazards in regards to food safety, and their presence can lead to the outbreak of serious foodborne diseases and promote the waste of food resources [1,2]. The quorum sensing (QS) system plays an important role in determining the virulence of foodborne pathogens and reflects the activity of spoilage bacteria [3,4]. In the QS system, the concentration of extracellular signal molecules is closely related to the cellular density of the bacteria, and the biological behavior among the bacteria is further regulated according to the cellular density [5]. N-acylated homoserine lactone (AHL), is a typical signaling molecular component produced by Gram-negative bacteria [6]. AHL functions to regulate the virulence genes of bacteria, and also recognizes eukaryotic cells and reduces their own immune responses [7]. In Pseudomonas aeruginosa the LasR protein is required for activation of lasB and several other virulence genes. The signaling molecules AHLs produced by bacterial cells and released into the growth medium are necessary for LasR activity [8]. The signaling molecule AHLs can block the cellular immune signaling pathway by inhibiting the mammalian cellular immune response or inflammatory response, which ultimately leads to apoptosis or necrosis [9]. In addition, it has been reported that the corruption of some foods is also regulated by the AHLs-mediated QS system. AHLs can be produced by food-related bacteria such as Serratia plymuthica, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Aeromonas hydrophila, Enterobacter agglomerans, and Yersinia enterocolitica [10], among which Serratia mainly produces two principal AHLs, N-hexanoyl-l-homoserine-lactone (C6-HSL) and N-3-oxo-hexanoyl homoserine lactone (3-oxo-C6-HSL) [11]. Pseudomonas aeruginosa also produces N-3-oxo-dodecanoyl-L-homoserine-lactone (3OC12-HSL) and N-butyryl-L-homoserine-lactone (C4-HSL) [12]. Some Gram-negative bacteria (Salmonella enterica and Escherichia coli) that don’t produce AHLs are also receptors for AHL signaling molecules, and these bacteria can alter their gene expression in response to AHLs produced by other Gram-negative flora [13,14].

The AHL signal molecule is a small molecule compound that is usually present at very low concentrations outside the cell, such as, the methods used to detect AHL require a high sensitivity. A number of different methods have been reported for detecting AHL signaling molecules, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS/MS), and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS) [15,16]. However, these methods are expensive, time-consuming, and require professional operators. In contrast, relatively simple and fast bacterial biosensor methods are not sensitive enough to accurately detect AHL, and this can lead to false-negative results due to their limited detection thresholds [17]. Therefore, it is critical to develop a method for rapid AHL detection with a high sensitivity, low cost, rapid response and simple operation.

Molecular imprinting is a technique used for preparing polymers with specific recognition sites that can specifically bind the template molecules. In recent years, molecular imprinting technology has become one of the most promising technologies in the field of sensor design because of its high selectivity, simplicity, rapidity, stability, low cost, and low environmental impact [18–20]. In general, template molecules are combined with functional monomers to form a complex with a unique spatial structure, the template molecules and functional monomers are formed into the polymers by adding a cross-linker and initiator. An eluant is then added to remove the template molecules, creating specific structural voids in the polymer that bind specifically to structural analogs of the template molecules [20,21]. Here, since AHLs are expensive and do not tolerate polymerization conditions, furanone, which is similar in size, shape and function to N-acylated homoserine lactones, was chosen as the template molecule to prepare the molecularly imprinted polymers [10,22].

As an emerging type of nano-material with a quantum size, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing attention. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CQDs have the advantages of a simple synthesis route, low toxicity, high stability, and environmental friendliness [23]. Therefore, CQDs have been used as a good substitutes for semiconductor QDs in many research fields [24–27]. At present, several kinds of molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors based on semiconductor QDs have been reported [28,29]. For example, our research group constructed an AHL detection sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer coated QDs [22], and Wang et al. successfully established a novel fluorescent probe for serotonin detection based on Mn-doped ZnS QDs@SiO2@MIPs [30]. However, when using these methods, the QDs require a complicated pretreatment to be used in polymerization, and heavy metal QDs add to the pollution of the environment as well as certain toxicological effects on humans. Therefore, the combination of CQDs with molecularly imprinted technology provides a kind of sensing material with low-toxicity, eco-friendly and rapid response towards the target. Importantly, the number of sensors that can be used to detect AHLs signals quickly and sensitively is currently extremely limited.

Herein, based on the molecular imprinting technique, an eco-friendly optical-sensing platform using long-wave emissions of CQDs as fluorescent indicator signals was constructed for rapid identification and quantification of AHLs. CQD-embedded fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific recognition sites were obtained via convenient and efficient sol-gel polymerization, and these were then characterized using UV spectroscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and fluorescence spectrophotometer. After the detection conditions were optimized, the CQD@MIPs were used for fluorescence detection of the AHLs target molecule, and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of the CQD@MIPs gradually decreased with the increase in AHLs concentration. To the best of our knowledge, molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors based on CQDs for the detection of AHLs have not been previously reported. In addition, the proposed detection strategy was used for the analysis of AHLs in bacterial supernatant samples and generated satisfactory results. Finally, 3D-printing CQD@MIPs were prepared, and its uniform structure made the decentralized CQD@MIPs integrated, which would be the in-situ sensing in the future.

Methacrylic acid (MAA), γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), and 2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). N-butyryl-L-Homoserine lactone (C4-HSL), N-hexanoyl-L-Homoserine lactone (C6-HSL), N-octanoyl-L-Homoserine lactone (C8-HSL), and N-(β-ketocaproyl)-L-Homoserine lactone (3-O-C6-(L)-HSL) were obtained from Cayman Chemical (Ann Arbor, MI, USA). 2, 5-dimethyl-4-hydroxy-3(2H)-furanone (DMHF) was supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China. Photo-crosslinkable gelatin methacryloyl (GelMA, EFL-GM-30) was acquired from Suzhou Intelligent Manufacturing Research Institute (Suzhou, China). All solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water from the Mill-Q-RO4 water purification system (Millipore).

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on an PERSEE-T9 dual beam UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was measured on a Thermo FT-IR spectrophotometer. The images of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were captured on a JEOL, JSM-7001F scanning electron microscope, (Tokyo, Japan). The EFL-BP-6800 desktop bioprinter (Suzhou, China) was used to printing GelMA ink.

CQDs were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method according to our previous work. Briefly, 0.09 g 2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water, and loaded into a Teflon autoclave (180 °C, 10 h) and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the suspension was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was dialyzed against a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 24 h to remove the raw material. A reddish-brown powder was obtained by freeze drying and was stored at 4 °C.

For the synthesis of the imprinted polymer, a previously reported method was used with some modification. Briefly, 0.5 mM of the analog template (DMHF), 2.0 mM of the monomer (MAA), and CQDs (5 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of a chloroform and acetonitrile (4:1 v/v) mixture and then sonicated for 10 min in the dark. After stirring at 800 rpm for 1 h, the cross-linker (MPTMS) and initiator (ABIN) were added, and the mixture was degassed through ultrasonication (10 min) and purged with N2 for 5 min. Subsequently, under sealed conditions, the mixture was stirred at 300 rpm in a water bath at 55 °C for 24 h. After polymerization, the precipitates were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, repeatedly washed with acetone to remove the unreacted reagents, and then repeatedly flushed with 0.4 M acidified acetone with ultrasound assistance to remove the template molecules until they could not be detected by UV–vis spectrophotometry, and the elution time for each time was 10 min. Finally, the CQD@MIPs were dried overnight at 55 °C under vacuum. Meanwhile, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP)-coated CQDs composites were synthesized following the same method but in the absence of template molecule.

The optical sensing properties of the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs were evaluated under the following conditions: the excitation wavelength was set at 500 nm, emission wavelength range was selected between 520 and 750 nm, slit width was 10 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage was 400 V. Typically, different concentrations of the DMHF diluent were prepared. A total of 15 mg of the CQD@MIPs or CQD@NIPs were dispersed into 10 mL of chloroform via ultrasonic vibration. Subsequently, 100 μL of different concentrations of the DMHF solution was added to 900 μL of the CQD@MIPs solution. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer to examine the optical sensing properties of the CQD@MIPs. In addition, the stock solutions were prepared separately by dissolving the N-acyl-l-homoserine lactones (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, 3-O-C6-(L)-HSL, C8-HSL) in DMSO, and this was followed by further dilutions of the stock solutions using chloroform. The adsorption and competitive recognition capabilities of the synthetic materials were then evaluated using the same procedure as before.

After the bacterial strain was activated twice on solid medium, a single colony was picked and inoculated into a liquid medium, and this was cultured overnight at 28 °C with shaking. To extract the AHLs, the culture solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain supernatants. The supernatants were then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain a sterile supernatant. The obtained supernatants were repeatedly extracted three times using a mixture of 20 mL ethyl acetate (added 0.5% acetic acid). The extracts were then collected together and dried under nitrogen with the help of a rotary evaporator, then followed by re-dissolving in 1 mL of acidified ethyl acetate and stored at −20 °C until tested for the presence of AHLs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CQD@MIPs sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic diagram depicting the construction of the CQD@MIPs sensing strategy is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, based on previous work, the yellow-emitting CQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method with 2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as the raw material. Some functionalized groups, such as amino groups exist on the surface of CQDs, which improve their optical performance and reactivity. In the case of CQD@MIP polymerization reaction, DMHF, MAA, MPTMS, and ABIN were used as the analog template, functional monomer, cross-linker, and catalyst, respectively. The carboxyl groups of the functional monomer interacted with the amino groups of the CQDs to form NH3 + and COO−. Furthermore, MAA could react with DMHF via hydrogen bonds and, thus, introduce the template to the surface of polymers. After the removal of the template molecules, specific imprinted cavities were successfully formed on the surface of the CQD@MIPs. Subsequently, the quantitative detection of the AHLs using CQD@MIPs demonstrated a good fluorescence response.

Long-wave-emitting CQDs served as fluorescent signal probes with excellent optical stability and anti-interference abilities. The fluorescence properties of CQDs dissolved in chloroform were explored. As shown in Fig. S1, it was found that the fluorescence emission of CQDs showed an excitation-dependent feature. The fluorescence emission peak of CQDs gradually red-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelength from 420 nm to 540 nm. Since the maximum of emission wavelength and considerable fluorescence intensity of CQDs were observed at an excitation wavelength of 500 nm, this wavelength was used as the excitation wavelength for all subsequent experiments. Fig. 1 (A) shows the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs and eluted CQD@MIPs at a 500 nm excitation wavelength. Compared with the CQDs, the CQD@MIPs emission position was red-shifted by 12 nm, which might have been due to the increase in the size of CQDs during the polymerization of MAA and MPTMS [31]. In order to further confirm that the CQDs were successfully embedded in the MIPs, the FT-IR absorption spectrums of CQDs (curve a), CQD@MIPs (curve b) and MIPs without embedded CQDs (curve c) were assessed and compared, as shown in Fig. 1 (B). The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs displayed three characteristic peaks representing the stretching vibration of O–H (3428 cm−1), N–H (3342 cm−1), and –SH (2609 cm−1), indicating that nitrogen and sulfur were successfully doped onto the CQDs. In addition, a weak peak at 3059 cm−1 and two characteristic peaks between 1650 and 1450 cm−1, indicating the presence of aromatic nucleus. As shown in the FT-IR spectra of CQD@MIPs, the broad absorption peak at 3411 cm−1 correspond to N–H stretching, the peak at 1556 cm−1 represented secondary amide N–H bending, and the peak at 1698 cm−1 corresponded to secondary amide CO stretching, which indicate the presence of amide bonding. However, the FT-IR spectra of MIPs without embedded CQDs failed to clearly observe the CQDs characteristic peaks of 2920 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1. Together, the above results further confirmed that the MIPs were successfully formed on the CQDs. To further study the fluorescence stability of the CQD@MIPs, the PL intensities are still above 90% after 12 h of illumination, indicating that CQD@MIPs exhibit excellent light stability (Fig. S2).

As performed in the report by Xu et al. [32], the template molecules were removed by ultrasonic assisted by repeated washing with 0.4 M acidified acetone. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the content of template molecules in the washing solvent gradually decreased as the elution time increased. After the fifth washing, the UV absorption peak of DMHF was not detected in the washing solvent. To ascertain the successful removal of the template molecule DMHF from the polymer, the FT-IR spectra of DMHF and CQD@MIPs before and after the template extraction were assessed. As shown in curve a of Fig. 2B, the characteristic absorption peaks of furanone were observed at 3169 cm−1 (O–H), 1686 cm−1 (CO), 1600 cm−1 (CC), and 1309 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 (C–O), respectively. These characteristic peaks were also observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CQD@MIPs (curve d of Fig. 2B) before removal of the template molecules, but these were mostly absent after solvent elution (curve c of Fig. 2B). In addition, it was found that the FT-IR spectrum of the CQD@NIPs (curve b of Fig. 2B) was the same as that of CQD@MIPs after solvent elution. This observation further indicated that the DMHF molecules were successfully blotted into the MIPs via polymerization and then effectively removed by the solvent elution process. According to Fig. 2C, the MIPs before the elution showed a weak fluorescent signal (curve e of Fig. 2C), however, after the template molecules were removed, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-coated CQD composites (curve c of Fig. 2C) had mostly recovered to that of CQD@NIPs (curve a of Fig. 2C). This result indicated that most of the template molecules were effectively removed from the MIPs. However, after the introduction of DMHF into the MIP system after solvent elution, the fluorescence intensity of composite decreased again (curve d of Fig. 2C), and this might have been caused by charge transfer between the CQDs and DMHF, which acted as electron donors and acceptors, respectively. Under the action of hydrogen bond, they are more accessible, which would be beneficial to the transfer of electrons. However, when DMHF is introduced into the CQD@NIPs system, the fluorescence intensity of the system only slightly decreased (curve b of Fig. 2C). This phenomenon might have been caused by the absence of the specific imprinted cavity on the surface of CQD@NIPs. Therefore, the obtained CQD@MIPs could be used to specifically identify DMHF.

The images generated via SEM representing the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs samples are presented in Fig. 3 (A) and (B). The inset images in Fig. 3 also revealed the different morphologies of the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs sample surfaces. The surface of CQD@MIPs was rough and presented a porous structure. The results show that the surface of CD@MIPs is rough and presents a porous structure, while the surface of CD@NIPs without template molecules is relatively smooth. The above results also indirectly indicate that the template molecules on the CD@MIPs polymer have been successfully eluted, and CD@MIPs can be further applied to the fluorescence detection of AHLs [33].

During the detection process, the concentration of fluorescent composite CQD@MIPs was speculated to be critical for the detection of target, as CQD@MIPs at an excessive concentration would reduce the detection sensitivity, and an insufficient concentration would affect the response range of the analyte. Hence, the detection ability of the DMHF was investigated using different concentrations of CQD@MIPs in the detection system. The sensitivity and linearity range of the test system was excellent at a concentration of 1.5 mg/mL (Fig. S3). Importantly, the reaction time is also an important indicator of the detection process. To test the reaction time, a DMHF standard solution was introduced into the CQD@MIPs test system (1.5 mg/mL), and the fluorescence intensity of the test solution was recorded at different time points. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the test solution decreased rapidly over the first 2 min and, thereafter, remained constant up to 5 min, indicating that the optimal reaction time was 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric sensing of analyte by CQD@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the above optimal test conditions, the quantitative detection performance of the CQD@MIPs towards DMHF was investigated. Different concentrations of DMHF were introduced into the CQD@MIPs test system, and the fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength of 500 nm, emission wavelength of 520–750 nm) were recorded after a 2 min incubation. Fig. 4 A and B presents the changes in fluorescence intensity of the CQD@MIPs and NIPs, respectively, in the presence of different concentrations of the analyte. As the concentration of DMHF in the test system increased, the fluorescence intensity of the MIPs and NIPs embedded in CQDs gradually decreased. However, the fluorescence intensity of the CQD@MIPs decreased more significantly than that of the CQD@NIPs, which is likely due to the large number of recognition sites complementary to DMHF on the MIP surface after elution of the template molecules. Moreover, these specific imprinting cavities strongly absorb DMHF via hydrogen bonding, which made the CQD@MIPs more sensitive to the target molecules. In contrast, the fluorescence quenching effect of DMHF on CQD@NIPs is very weak (Fig. 4B), which may be due to the only non-specific adsorption between them. The fluorescence quenching properties can be quantified using the Stern-Volmer equation, which is expressed as follows [34]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
C
D M H F
Where F
0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CQD@MIPs in the absence and presence of the analyte (DMHF), respectively, K
SV represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C
DMHF represents the concentration of the analyte. The ratio of the K
SV constants of the MIPs and NIPs were defined as the imprinted factor (IF) to evaluate the selectivity. The different K
SV constants in these experiments could be calculated using this equation.

Fig. 4C, drawn from the Stern-Volmer equation, indicates a good linear relationship between the concentration of F
0 /F and DMHF. From the linear equation, K
SV, MIP was calculated to be 0.223 μM−1, the linear range of the calibration curve was 0–2.0 μM, and the linear correlation coefficient was 0.97359. In contrast, the value of K
SV, NIP was 0.062 μM−1, the linear range of the calibration curve was 0–2.0 μM, and the correlation coefficient was 0.98346. The ratio of K
SV, MIP to K
SV, NIP was 3.597 (IF), which indicated that the MIPs had excellent selectivity towards DMHF. In addition, the LOD for DMHF by CQD@MIPs was calculated to be 0.067 μM according to three times the signal-to-noise criteria. The detection performance relative to several typical AHLs (C4-HSL, 3-O-C6-(L)-HSL, C6-HSL, and C8-HSL) is summarized in Table 1 .

Molecular imprinting is a versatile technique for constructing specific recognition sites, and it is extremely important to ensure its selectivity. Therefore, the recognition performance of the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs towards several AHLs with chemical structures similar to the template molecule (DMHF) was investigated. The fluorescence quenching effect of several AHLs on the CQD@MIPs was mostly similar to that of the template molecules. In contrast, their effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQD@NIPs was almost negligible. To further investigate the selectivity of the CQD@MIPs towards AHLs, the corresponding K
sv constants and IFs for the different AHLs were calculated based on Stern-Volmer relationships, as shown in Table 1. The results indicated that the designed fluorescence sensor possessed a good response towards the target (AHL) and could effectively recognize AHL. Subsequently, the effects of several other compounds related to DMHF on the fluorescence of the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs were assessed. The structural formula of these kinds of lactone molecules is shown in Fig. S5. Compared with the AHLs, the fluorescence quenching effect of these compounds on CQD@MIPs was much weaker, which indicated that the imprinting cavity on CQD@MIPs could not effectively bind these compounds, which included maltol, phthalide, and tremetone. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching effects of these compounds towards the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs were similar, in that only non-specific binding occurred between them and the CQD@MIPs and CQD@NIPs (Fig. 5 A). Therefore, the recognition site of the CQD@MIPs could not specifically bind to other related compounds and only produced a suitable fluorescence response to AHLs with a similar structure to that of DMHF.

In addition, several different types of bacteria were cultured for further verification of the actual working domain of the fluorescence probe, and the presence of AHL in supernatant solution was assessed using different concentrations of the above bacterial solutions. Among these, E. Coli and Staphylococcus aureus represent two kinds of bacteria that cannot produce AHL, but they can respond to the presence of AHL. The quantitative results regarding AHL in the bacterial supernatants are shown in Fig. 5B. Except for E. coli and S. aureus, which cannot produce AHL, all of the other bacteria produced a fluorescence response during the tests. The above results indicated that the constructed fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor could selectively detect AHL in bacterial supernatants.

In order to evaluate the potential value of CQD@MIPs as fluorescent recognition probes, the following method was used to determine the AHL content in the bacterial supernatants. After the bacterial supernatant was pretreated, four typical Gram-negative signal molecules, C4-HSL, 3-O-C6-(L)-HSL, C6-HSL and C8-HSL, were added to the bacterial supernatant at concentrations of 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 μM, respectively, this method was then used to analyze the samples. The analysis results are summarized in Table S1. The relative standard deviation of the detection results of the four typical Gram-negative signal molecules spiked samples was less than 2.32, and the spiked recovery range was 98.20–110.8%. These results indicated that the sensor platform possessed a good performance. Table S2 summarized many other excellent works have been reported about the AHLs detection and compared with this work. Although the complicated, high-cost and time-consuming HPLC-MS/MS methods demonstrated the ideal LOD, the CQD@MIPs sensor provided a simple and rapid detection strategy for AHLs in real sample. Therefore, the proposed fluorescence sensor is more suitable and promising for the on-site AHLs detection.

Based on the CQD@MIPs with excellent fluorescence response to the target, which prompted us to further investigate the visual sensing performance of CQD@MIPs. The photo-crosslinked methacrylylated gelatin mixed with CQD@MIPs was used as printing ink and a specific model was printed using an extrusion 3D bioprinter for visually detecting template molecules. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B preliminary sensing experiments of the 3D-printing CQD@MIPs were conducted, the fluorescence intensity of DMHF incubated with 10 μM aqueous solution decreased under the illumination of a 450 nm LED lamp and pure water was used as control in this experiment. Thus, 3D-printing CQD@MIPs could be used for DMHF sensing and its stable structure was easy to take and store, which make it a powerful approach in point of care testing (POCT).

In conclusion, this study presented the fabrication of an eco-friendly molecularly imprinted composite based on long-wavelength emitting CQDs via a facile and effective sol-gel method, and this composite could be used for the rapid fluorescent detection of AHLs, a typical auto-inducer produced by Gram-negative bacteria. Importantly, this novel fluorescent-sensing platform demonstrated prominent merits, including environmental friendliness, low cost, stable fluorescence, rapid response, and good selectivity. Additionally, the CQD@MIP sensor demonstrated a low detection limit and was used in the analysis of AHLs in bacterial supernatants with satisfactory results. The 3D-printing CQD@MIPs were firstly used for visual detection of AHLs based on 3D printing technology, its facility and utility make MIPs easier than current method, which is good at point of care testing (POCT) with visual fluorescence. Based on the above results, the developed sensing platform revealed its substantial potential for use in food safety and healthcare facilities.

Xingxing Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing-Original Draft. Jiadi Sun: Methodology, Writing-Review & Editing. Fangchao Cui: Data curation. Jian Ji: Investigation, Writing-Review & Editing. Liping Wang: Resources. Yinzhi Zhang: Project administration. Xiulan Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors declare that he had no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the National Research Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602800), Jiangsu Agriculture Innovation Fund (CX (17) 3007), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190584), Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Dairy Biotechnology (No. SKLDB2019-005), National First-class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180303), Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control.
